era_context: The early 10th century CE continues the fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate and the rise of regional Islamic dynasties. In China, the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period persists, with the Later Liang Dynasty controlling northern China. The Byzantine Empire under Constantine VII is consolidating power. In Western Europe, feudal kingdoms are strengthening, and the Ottonian Saxon dynasty is rising in the East Frankish realm. The Khmer Empire in Southeast Asia is at a peak of cultural and political power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Scandinavia (sparse documentary evidence)
Americas (no written records; archaeological evidence limited)

events.title: Zhu Yougui becomes emperor of Later Liang Dynasty
events.description: Following the death of Zhu Wen (Taizu) in 912 CE, his son Zhu Yougui ascends to the throne as the second emperor of the Later Liang Dynasty. Zhu Yougui's reign is marked by internal instability and challenges to his authority from military generals and regional warlords. His rule is relatively brief and troubled, lasting only about a year before his assassination in 913 CE. The succession crisis within Later Liang contributes to the dynasty's eventual collapse and replacement by the Later Tang Dynasty in 923 CE.
key_figures: Zhu Yougui (Emperor Yin of Later Liang, r. 912–913)
Zhu Wen (predecessor, founder of Later Liang)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang
Official histories of the Five Dynasties (Wu Dai Shi)

events.certainty_note: The succession of Zhu Yougui and his brief reign are well-documented in Chinese historical records. The date 912–913 CE is firmly established.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate faces continued fragmentation and regional challenges
events.description: In 913 CE, the Abbasid Caliphate, once the dominant Islamic power, continues to face fragmentation and loss of territorial control. Regional dynasties—including the Fatimids in North Africa, the Buyids in Persia, and the Umayyads in al-Andalus—have established independent or semi-independent rule. The Abbasid Caliph in Baghdad retains symbolic religious authority but limited political power. The Caliphate's military and economic resources are severely diminished. This period marks the transition from a centralized Islamic empire to a multipolar Islamic world, with power distributed among regional dynasties.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)

events.certainty_note: The fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate in the early 10th century is well-attested in Islamic sources and modern scholarship. The process is gradual, and 913 CE is an arbitrary point within a broader trend.
cross_references: Contemporary with Fatimid rise (909 CE onward)
Precedes Buyid rise to prominence (945 CE)

events.title: Byzantine military campaigns in the Balkans and Aegean
events.description: Under Constantine VII, the Byzantine Empire conducts military campaigns to consolidate control over the Balkans and the Aegean. These campaigns are part of a broader effort to restore Byzantine authority in regions threatened by Bulgarian expansion and Arab raids. The Byzantine military, reorganized and strengthened during this period, achieves mixed results but gradually extends Byzantine influence. These campaigns lay the groundwork for later Byzantine military successes under Basil II in the late 10th century.
key_figures: Romanus I Lecapenus (co-emperor and military strategist, from 920)

sources.name: De Administrando Imperio by Constantine VII

events.certainty_note: Byzantine military activity in the Balkans during the early 10th century is attested in sources, but specific campaigns for 913 CE are not always clearly dated. The entry reflects the broader context of Byzantine military consolidation.
cross_references: Part of Macedonian Renaissance in Byzantine Empire (9th–11th centuries)
Precedes Basil II's Balkan campaigns (late 10th century)

events.title: Goryeo Kingdom consolidates control of Korean Peninsula
events.description: The Goryeo Kingdom, founded in 918 CE by Wang Geon, is in its early years of consolidation. By 913 CE, the kingdom is establishing administrative structures and asserting control over the Korean Peninsula, replacing the fragmented Later Three Kingdoms period (892–935 CE). Goryeo's rise represents a major political reorganization of the peninsula and the beginning of a dynasty that will rule Korea for nearly 500 years (918–1392 CE). The kingdom adopts Buddhist culture and establishes diplomatic relations with neighboring states.
key_figures: Wang Geon (Taejo of Goryeo, founder, r. 918–943)

sources.name: Samguk Sagi (History of the Three Kingdoms) by Kim Busik

events.certainty_note: The founding of Goryeo is traditionally dated to 918 CE, but the consolidation of control over the peninsula took several years. The year 913 CE falls within the early consolidation period, though specific events for this year are not well-documented.
cross_references: Part of Later Three Kingdoms period (892–935)

events.title: Arab-Byzantine naval conflicts in the Mediterranean
events.region: Mediterranean Sea, Byzantine Empire, Arab Caliphates
events.description: Throughout the early 10th century, Arab and Byzantine naval forces contest control of the Mediterranean. Arab fleets, based in North Africa and the Levant, raid Byzantine coastal territories and merchant shipping. Byzantine naval forces, strengthened during this period, conduct counter-raids and attempt to protect trade routes. These conflicts are part of the long-standing Arab-Byzantine struggle for Mediterranean dominance that has persisted since the 7th century. The balance of power shifts gradually in favor of the Byzantines during the 10th century.
key_figures: Byzantine and Arab naval commanders (largely unrecorded)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and coastal sites

events.certainty_note: Arab-Byzantine naval conflicts in the Mediterranean during the early 10th century are well-attested in general terms, but specific events for 913 CE are not clearly documented. The entry reflects the broader context of Mediterranean naval warfare.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding date of Goryeo is traditionally placed in 918 CE, not 913 CE; the entry reflects the early consolidation period, which is less well-documented. Specific military campaigns by Constantine VII in 913 CE are not clearly attested in surviving sources; the entry generalizes from broader 10th-century patterns. The significance of events in 913 CE is difficult to establish for most regions; the year is not marked by major documented turning points in most historical traditions.
historiographic_note: The year 913 CE is moderately well-documented in Chinese, Byzantine, and Islamic sources, but these are primarily later compilations rather than contemporary records. Western European documentation is sparse. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Islamic sources tend to emphasize the Abbasid perspective. Chinese sources are abundant but focus on dynastic politics. Byzantine sources are relatively rich but concentrate on Constantinople and the Mediterranean. East Asian sources (Korean, Japanese) are less abundant in Western scholarship but provide important regional context.

graph_edges.note: Zhu Yougui's succession follows from Zhu Wen's death in 912 CE
Zhu Yougui's brief and troubled reign contributes to Later Liang's collapse and Later Tang's rise
Goryeo's consolidation in 913 CE is part of the broader founding process that culminates in 918 CE
Abbasid fragmentation in 913 CE precedes the rise of the Buyid Dynasty to prominence in 945 CE
